verses.commentary: The invitation to salvation. Righteousness is relative to his generation, not absolute perfection. God sees Noah's heart.
Clean animals come in larger numbers—seven pairs for sacrifice and food. The distinction between clean and unclean predates the Mosaic Law.
Birds receive special attention for preservation. Species continuity matters to God.
One week warning, then forty days and nights. Forty becomes a biblical pattern for judgment and testing (Moses on Sinai, Elijah's journey, Jesus in wilderness). The number suggests completeness.
Perfect obedience. The refrain emphasizes Noah's faithfulness.
Chronological marker. Noah has lived 600 years before this climactic moment.
Eight people total. Peter later calls Noah a preacher of righteousness (2 Peter 2:5). For 120 years Noah built and warned. Only his family listened.
Creation cooperates with God's plan. The animals come willingly.
Orderly procession. God orchestrates the rescue; Noah obeys.
The countdown ends. Judgment arrives on schedule. God's word proves true.
Precise dating. The springs of the deep (tehom, תְּהוֹם, same word from Genesis 1:2) burst forth. Waters above and below reunite. The separation of Genesis 1:6-7 reverses. Creation undoes itself.
The deluge begins. Water from above and below converge. Total inundation.
Repetition for emphasis. The eight are named again. They are safe inside before the deluge.
Comprehensive list. The ark contains a microcosm of creation—all types present.
The breath of life echoes Genesis 2:7. These are living beings, not just biological specimens.
God closes the door. This is both protection and sealing. Those inside are secure; those outside have no access. Salvation has a boundary.
The ark rises on waters of judgment. Those inside experience salvation; those outside, destruction.
The ark functions as designed. God's provision works perfectly.
Total inundation. The highest peaks submerged. No escape by climbing.
About twenty-two feet above the mountains. Complete coverage ensures nothing survives outside the ark.
Universal death. The undoing of creation. All flesh dies.
The breath that God gave (2:7) is withdrawn. Life returns to lifelessness.
Total judgment, singular salvation. Only eight humans survive. The ark alone preserves life. God's grace is specific and decisive.
Five months under water. The earth remains under judgment. Waiting is part of salvation.
